title: Lemon posset
subtitle: Cream set with lemon juice, no gelatine.
excerpt: Double cream heated with sugar, sharpened with lemon juice, and poured into glasses to set. No gelatine, no eggs; the lemon acid sets the cream directly. Three ingredients and 15 minutes of active time. Chill for four hours. Serves six.
sourceNotes: Posset is an ancient British drink of hot milk curdled with wine or ale; the modern lemon posset set with cream is a 20th-century British restaurant invention, though the principle of acid-set cream appears in earlier dairy science literature. References to cream posset appear in Eliza Acton's Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845, Project Gutenberg) and earlier household management texts.

recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly. Make it the night before for effortless entertaining.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the day before and refrigerate overnight. Cover each glass with cling film. The texture firms slightly as it chills longer.

content.text: Three ingredients. Double cream, caster sugar, lemon juice. The cream is brought to the boil with the sugar, taken off the heat, and the lemon juice stirred in. The acid sets the cream as it cools, no gelatine required. Poured into small glasses and chilled for four hours, it comes out as a smooth, silky, intensely lemony cream that wobbles when you move the glass but slices cleanly with a spoon.

The margin for error is small. Too little lemon and it won't set; too much and it tastes raw and sharp. The ratio here is tested: juice of {{lemon}} lemons to {{double-cream}} of cream. Use a digital scale for the juice, not a rough squeeze.

items.prepNote: juice only, about 90 ml; measure the juice carefully

content.text: Put {{double-cream}} of double cream and {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar into a heavy saucepan. Bring to a full rolling boil over medium heat, stirring to dissolve the sugar. The cream will foam up; stir it back. Boil for exactly 3 minutes, watching closely to prevent it boiling over.
Remove the pan from the heat. Pour in all the juice from {{lemon}} lemons and stir well. You will see the cream thicken slightly as the acid hits. Leave to cool for 5 minutes, then stir once more.

Skim any skin from the surface of the warm posset. Pour into six small glasses (150 ml capacity each) or ramekins, filling to 1 cm below the rim. Leave to cool to room temperature, then refrigerate for at least 4 hours until set.

items.symptom: Posset didn't set and is still liquid after 4 hours
items.cause: Not enough lemon juice, or the cream didn't boil for long enough.
items.fix: Measure the juice precisely. Boil for the full 3 minutes; a shorter boil reduces the cream less and the acid ratio works out wrong.

items.cause: Too much lemon juice, or juice added while the cream was too hot.
items.fix: Let the pan cool off the heat for 30 seconds before adding the juice. Measure carefully and don't exceed the stated amount.

items.cause: Lemons weren't acidic enough, or insufficient juice.
items.fix: Add more lemon juice next time. Supermarket lemons vary; taste the juice before you measure and adjust slightly.

items.cause: Normal, and happens as the cream cools.
items.fix: Skim before pouring into glasses. If a skin forms in the glass, peel it off with a small spoon before serving.

content.text: Lemon posset became a British restaurant staple in the 1980s and 1990s, popular in part because of how effortlessly it can be made ahead. It sits at the elegant and simple end of the dessert spectrum: nothing to fold, no temperature monitoring, no water bath. It pairs well with shortbread biscuits on the side, or with a scatter of fresh raspberries on top.
